commit.message:
Precise array shift invalidation for list mutations

Add shifted_from field to MutationTrie for range-based proxy
invalidation.  Mid-array insert, remove, pop, and slice operations now
only invalidate proxies at or after the shift point — earlier proxies
survive.

- Add Affected enum (Child vs Shift) replacing the overloaded
  Option<Key> return type from list mutation helpers
- Add Affected::for_removal() constructor to deduplicate the
  end-of-array optimisation across Array and AoT arms
- item_insert returns Option<Affected> for consistency
- stamp_shift on MutationTrie accumulates by taking min(threshold)
- stamp() clears shifted_from since revised_at subsumes it
- Slice deletion uses bump_shift(min_idx); slice assignment uses
  bump_shift(start)
- 7 new trie unit tests, 8 new Python staleness tests

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
fix: add staleness checks to KeysView, ValuesView, ItemsView

Views now store a creation revision and check the mutation trie before
every access, matching the existing ItemProxy staleness model.

Previously, accessing a view whose path had been replaced (e.g.
`view = doc["foo"].keys(); doc["foo"] = "bar"; list(view)`)
raised a confusing TypeError ("string does not support keys()").
Now it raises RuntimeError("stale"), consistent with how proxies
behave.

The staleness check is unified in Document.check_fresh(), used by both
ItemProxy and views, with a single consistent error message.

Root-level views (doc.keys()) remain live for child mutations since
the root path is never invalidated — only doc.clear() makes them stale.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
refactor: deduplicate array removal logic in list_ops

item_remove_at is now the single implementation for removing an element
from an array-like TOML item (Array or ArrayOfTables) with correct
decor handling.

- list_pop delegates to item_remove_at after resolving the index.
- item_delitem (Array/AOT branches) delegates via as_array_like_mut,
  collapsing two match arms into one.
- save_removal_decor is called with the correct booleans directly
  instead of saving both decorations then nullifying the unneeded ones.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Add 12 platform-specific IPC examples (#64)

* Add file-watcher example with inotify-backed server and tests

Linux directory watcher using inotify that demonstrates IPC notifications.
Server watches directories and broadcasts FileChanged events to clients.
Seven integration tests cover watch/unwatch lifecycle, event notifications
for file creation/modification/deletion, and error handling.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* Add sysmon example with /proc-based CPU, memory, and load monitoring

Server reads /proc/stat, /proc/meminfo, and /proc/loadavg to implement
GetCpuUsage, GetMemoryInfo, and GetLoadAverage RPCs. A monitoring thread
checks thresholds every second and fires HighCpuAlert/LowMemoryAlert
notifications.

Client queries all three methods in a formatted table and listens for
threshold notifications. Test suite covers all RPCs, both notification
types, invalid method handling, and RunLoop dispatch mode (8 tests).

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* Add disk-usage example with simulated volumes

Implement the disk-usage example under examples/macos/disk-usage/:

- disk_device.cpp: Simulated server with 3 volumes (/, /home, /tmp).
  Usage on / grows 1 GB every 3s; fires DiskSpaceLow when > 90%.
- disk_client.cpp: Interactive CLI with list, check <mount>, quit
  commands and bar-chart display. Prints notification alerts.
- disk_test.cpp: 8 Google Test cases covering GetVolumeCount,
  GetVolumeAt (valid/invalid), GetDiskUsage (known/unknown mount),
  DiskSpaceLow notification, UsedPercent consistency, RunLoop dispatch.
- CMakeLists.txt: Builds device, client, and test executables.
- README.md: IDL overview, build/run instructions, sample output.

All volumes are simulated — no platform-specific APIs — so the example
compiles and runs on Linux, macOS, and Windows.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Implement server (dsp_device), client CLI (dsp_dashboard), and 12 Google
Test cases covering slot management, parameter updates, effect counting,
signal levels, invalid-slot errors, and ClipDetected/LevelUpdate
notifications.

ecu_dashboard (interactive CLI client), and ecu_test (11 Google
Test cases covering all RPC methods, notifications, and RunLoop
dispatch). Cross-platform — compiles on Linux and Windows.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* ci: move gcov detection into CMake coverage-report target via bilke/cmake-modules

Replace the fragile shell-side CMakeCache.txt parsing (grep | cut, subject
to SIGPIPE and set-e failures) with a cmake/Coverage.cmake module that
detects gcov/llvm-cov at CMake configure time—where CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER and
CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER_ID are natively available.

cmake/Coverage.cmake (new):
- Uses FetchContent to pull bilke/cmake-modules (pinned SHA 5b988b5) and
  includes CodeCoverage.cmake from that repository.
- Detects gcov (GCC) or llvm-cov (Clang) via find_program with HINTS from
  the compiler's own directory, avoiding version mismatches with system
  /usr/bin/gcov-* binaries.
- Pre-sets GCOVR_PATH to the bare "gcovr" command so CodeCoverage's
  FATAL_ERROR does not fire at configure time; gcovr is installed into a
  venv and placed on PATH before the target is invoked.
- Passes --gcov-executable, source --filter patterns, and --json-summary
  via GCOVR_ADDITIONAL_ARGS (each element is one VERBATIM shell argument;
  the space in "llvm-cov gcov" is intentional and handled by gcovr's
  shlex.split).
- Defines a coverage-report target via setup_target_for_coverage_gcovr_html
  that runs ctest (excluding dd4hep label) and gcovr, producing
  build/coverage-report/ and build/coverage-summary.json.
- Adds -fno-inline -fno-omit-frame-pointer alongside the module's standard
  --coverage -g -fprofile-abs-path flags.

CMakeLists.txt:
- Replace the inline add_compile_options/add_link_options block inside
  if(USE_COVERAGE) with include(cmake/Coverage.cmake).

.github/workflows/ci.yml:
- Drop ~50 lines of fragile gcov-detection shell code.
- Install gcovr into a venv, then invoke
  PATH=".venv-gcovr/bin:$PATH" cmake --build build --target coverage-report
- Update artifact and JSON summary paths from workspace-root coverage/ and
  coverage-summary.json to build/coverage-report/ and build/coverage-summary.json.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(cmake/Coverage): avoid GCOV_PATH-NOTFOUND overwrite in CodeCoverage

When find_program(GCOV_PATH llvm-cov ...) fails it writes GCOV_PATH-NOTFOUND
into the CMake cache.  The subsequent set(GCOV_PATH ... CACHE ...) without
FORCE does not override a cached NOTFOUND entry, so CodeCoverage.cmake's own
find_program(GCOV_PATH NAMES gcov) re-runs, also fails, and FATAL_ERRORs.

Fix:
- Use private _g4occt_* variables for detection so GCOV_PATH is never
  left as NOTFOUND before include(CodeCoverage).
- Always set GCOV_PATH with FORCE so CodeCoverage's check passes.
- For Clang when llvm-cov is not found, fall back to system gcov
  (Clang's --coverage flag generates GCOV-compatible .gcno/.gcda files).

* docs(squad): formalize PR review gate protocol — CI → review → fix cycle → merge

Per Matthew's directive: all PRs must pass CI, receive unanimous team review
(Aragorn + domain specialists), complete a fix cycle if rejected, then squash
merge with post-merge local main sync. Domain specialists based on PR content:
- DevOps/CI changes → Boromir
- Security changes → Gandalf
- Test changes → Gimli/Pippin
- Backend changes → Sam
- Frontend changes → Legolas

Lockout rule enforced: rejected artifacts cannot be self-revised; fixes route
to a different team member.

commit.message:
Add SEC 301 Cybersecurity Fundamentals course content

Create 4 college-level lesson files and 2 assignment files for the
Cybersecurity Fundamentals (SEC 301) course at LinuxNLearn.

Lessons added (content/cybersecurity/):
- 01_threat_landscape.yaml: CIA triad, MITRE ATT&CK, OWASP Top 10,
  attack types (phishing, SQLi, XSS, buffer overflow, MITM, DDoS,
  ransomware), threat actors, CVE/CVSS scoring, defense-in-depth,
  Linux recon commands, Mermaid diagrams, 5-question quiz
- 02_cryptography_pki.yaml: AES/DES/3DES, RSA, ECC, SHA-256/MD5,
  digital signatures, PKI chain of trust, TLS 1.3 handshake,
  X.509 certs, openssl CLI, Python cryptography library examples,
  Mermaid sequence diagram, 5-question quiz
- 03_network_security.yaml: Firewall architectures, iptables/nftables
  rules, IDS vs IPS, Snort/Suricata, VPNs (IPsec/OpenVPN/WireGuard),
  DMZ design, VLAN security, 802.1X, Wireshark, fail2ban, CIS
  hardening checklist, Mermaid diagrams, 5-question quiz
- 04_ethical_hacking.yaml: Pentest methodology (PTES 5 phases), legal
  framework (CFAA/CMA), OSINT, nmap, Nikto/OpenVAS, Metasploit
  architecture, privilege escalation, report writing, responsible
  disclosure, bug bounty, CEH/OSCP certification paths, 5-question quiz

Assignments added (assignments/cybersecurity/):
- 01_threat_landscape_assignment.yaml: beginner, 30 min,
  2 MC + 2 short_answer + 1 practical = 100 pts
- 02_network_security_assignment.yaml: intermediate, 40 min,
  2 MC + 2 short_answer + 1 practical = 100 pts

All files follow the exact YAML schema of existing course content.
All 6 files pass yaml.safe_load() validation.

feat: implement Pattern Detector (Phase 3)

After 20+ trades, Coach AI automatically detects patterns and displays insights:

1. Emotion & Discipline Analysis:
   - Identifies which emotional states lead to undisciplined trading
   - Alerts when discipline rate drops below 60% in specific emotions

2. Dangerous Sequences Detection:
   - Detects current losing streaks (2+ consecutive losses)
   - Warns about max loss streaks in last 20 trades
   - Suggests personal limits (stop after 2 losses)

3. Best Trading Conditions:
   - Finds markets with highest win rate (60%+ threshold)
   - Correlates positive emotions with winning trades
   - Shows optimal conditions for performance

4. Revenge Trading Detection:
   - Identifies patterns of trading after losses on same day
   - Flags trades with revenge/impulsive/FOMO emotions
   - Warns if 3+ revenge trades detected

Insights display in dashboard with visual cards:
- Warning cards (red) for alerts
- Success cards (green) for strengths
- Info cards (purple) for patterns

feat: validate runtime command values in syu.yaml at config load time (#88)

Reject runtimes.python.command and runtimes.node.command values that
look like shell expressions.  The validator rejects any value that:
- Contains shell metacharacters (; | & $ ` ! () {} <> ' " \)
- Contains whitespace (spaces or newlines)
- Is an absolute path that does not point to an existing file

'auto' (and empty string) is always accepted.  Bare executable names
such as 'python3', 'node', 'bun' pass through unchanged; PATH search
happens later in resolve_runtime_command.

The error is surfaced with context pointing to the syu.yaml path so
users can identify and fix the offending value immediately.

Closes #68

commit.message:
fix: make category filter apply consistently to all charts

YoYChart was using a separate categoryFilteredData memo that ignored
the year filter, causing year+category combinations to show mismatched
data across charts. Remove the redundant memo and pass filteredData
(year + category filtered) to all charts uniformly.

commit.message:
ci: auto-add CSV data from labeled GitHub issues

- Triggers on 'data-submission' label applied to any issue
- Parses Company, Layoffs, Date, Location, Source from issue body
- Appends new row to public/data/layoffs.csv on a feature branch
- Opens a PR and comments back on the issue with the PR link
